title: Conducting polymers: advanced materials for new design, rechargeable lithium batteries
abstract:
Polymer International 47 (1998) 50È55
Conducting Polymers: Advanced
Materials for New Design, Rechargeable
Lithium Batteries
Bruno Scrosati
Dipartimento di Chimica, Sezione di Elettrochimica, Universita` “La SapienzaÏ, 00185 Rome, Italy
(Received 4 October 1997; accepted 21 January 1998)
Abstract: The results obtained in our laboratory on the characterization of two
families of ionically-conducting membranes, i.e. poly(ethylene oxide)-based and
gel-type polymer electrolytes, are reviewed here. Particular attention is devoted
to the description of the conditions of the lithium electrode interface and to the
approaches which may be undertaken to improve its stability. The most prom-
ising applications of the two classes of polymer electrolytes are also outlined. (
1998 Society of Chemical Industry
Polym. Int. 47, 50È55 (1998)
Key words: polymers; ionic conductivity; electrolytes; interfacial properties;
polymer batteries
INTRODUCTION
The use of conducting polymers as electrolyte or elec-
trode materials for the development of plastic-like elec-
trochemical devices is an appealing concept which dates
back to the late 1960s. In the late 1970s Armand et al.1
showed that ionically conducting polymers, usually
called polymer electrolytes, could successfully replace
common liquid solutions as electrolytes in rechargeable
lithium polymer batteries, and Shirakawa et al.2 demon-
strated that electronically conducting polymers, such as
poly(acetylene) (pAc) poly(pyrrole) pPy) or poly(aniline)
(pAni) commonly called polymer electrodes could be
cycled in lithium batteries as well as the common in-
organic oxide intercalation cathodes.
Following these pioneering works, the interest in con-
ducting polymers has constantly increased due to the
inherent potentialities of these materials for important
technological innovations. The replacement of liquid
Contract/grant sponsor: International Joint Research Pro-
gramme, NEDO, Japan.
Contract/grant sponsor: ALPE Project, ENEA, Italy.
Contract/grant sponsor: Progetto Strategico Batterie Leggere,
CNR, Italy.
solutions and/or of inorganic solids with, respectively,
ionically and electronically-conducting membranes has
opened the route for the fabrication of devices of
growing importance in the consumer market. Relevant
examples
include
long-lasting
and
reduced-volume
lithium batteries designed for cellular phones and note-
books,3,4 redox type laminated supercapacitors,5 multi-
chromatic
optical
windows6,7
and
high-energy,
rechargeable
batteries
for
electric
vehicle
applica-
tions.8,9 Accordingly, many current research and devel-
opment projects are devoted to the characterization and
exploitation of various types of conducting polymers,
especially the ionic ones. In this paper, some results
obtained in our laboratory on the characterization of
two families of polymer electrolytes are reviewed and
discussed.
POLY(ETHYLENE OXIDE)-BASED
ELECTROLYTES
Complexes of lithium salts (LiX) with high molecular
weight poly(ethylene) oxide (PEO) form a very well-
known class of polymer electrolytes.10 These PEOÈLiX
50

Printed in Great Britain
(
 Conducting polymers for rechargeable L i batteries
51
electrolytes are commonly prepared by a casting pro-
cedure which uses acetonitrile as the carrier solvent.
Due to their particular structure, the conductivity of the
PEOÈLiX complexes becomes appreciable only at tem-
peratures above ambient, typically at around 100¡C.
This is, of course, a drawback for applications in the
consumer electronic market, but it is an acceptable limi-
tation for use in batteries designed for electric vehicles.
In fact, “trueÏ (i.e. plasticiser-free) PEOÈLiX electrolytes
are now being studied for this type of application.11 To
be competitive with other advanced battery systems, the
PEOÈLiX-based batteries must be structured in such a
way as to o†er a high energy density. This may be
achieved with the use of metallic lithium as the pre-
ferred anode and a suitable intercalation compound
(e.g.,
or
as cathode.
V6O13 , TiS2
MnO2)
While the compatibility of the PEOÈLiX electrolytes
with the cathode materials has been ascertained, there is
still some concern on the reactivity of these electrolytes
with the lithium metal anode. In fact, various studies
have demonstrated that lithium does react with the
polymer electrolyte with the formation of a surface
passivation layer, the nature and characteristics of
which vary according to the type of lithium salt and,
especially, according to the presence of liquid impu-
rities.12h14 This is not surprising, because the passiva-
tion of lithium is a well-known phenomenon which
unavoidably occurs in almost all types of electrolytes,
regardless of whether they are solid, liquid or polymeric.
Thus, the sole solution to the problem is that of selec-
ting the electrolyte which induces the most favourable
type of passivation layer, namely the layer which may
assure an efficient lithium deposition-stripping process.
This approach has been extensively pursued in liquid
electrolytes and accordingly, preferred solutions, usually
based on Ñuoride-containing lithium salts in ether-
based organic solvent mixtures, are presently proposed
for the development of safe and reliable rechargeable
lithium batteries.15,16
A similar type of approach should also be valid for
PEOÈLiX polymer electrolytes. However, in this case
the degree of choice is obviously much more restricted
because the solvent (PEO) is unchangeable and the
addition of third components, such as
or HF,18
CO217
which have proved to be very beneÐcial for promoting
the desired lithium passivation layers in liquid electro-
lytes, is very difficult, if not impossible, to achieve in
solid polymer electrolytes.
Composite PEO–LiX polymer electrolytes
In view of the difficulties outlined above for promoting
the conditions of an efficient lithium interface in
PEOÈLiX polymer electrolytes, we have considered
alternative approaches to those normally adopted in
liquid electrolytes. An e†ective one is that of dispersing
low-particle size ceramic powders in the electrolyte
bulk.19 This procedure gives composite polymer elec-
trolytes with improved lithium interfacial stability and
also with a slight increase in the overall ionic conduc-
tivity. A possible explanation for the enhancement of
the conductivity is the promotion of a high degree of
polymer amorphicity around the ceramic particles.20
The reason for the stabilization of the interface is not
yet fully clear. Possibly, the stabilizing e†ect may be
associated with the affinity of the powders for water and
for other liquid impurities which would then be trapped
and thus removed from the interface, this ultimately
favouring the formation of compact and uniform passi-
vation Ðlms on the lithium electrode interface. The most
active powders include alumina, lithium gamma alu-
minate and zeolites.
Figure 1 shows a comparison of the evolution of the
lithium interfacial resistance
(determined by complex
Ri
impedance analysis) in a plain
electrolyte
PEOÈLiClO4
and in two
composite electrolytes, one
PEOÈLiClO4
using lithium c-aluminate and the other zeolite, as the
powder additive. The Ðgure clearly shows that, while in
the plain electrolyte the interfacial resistance progres-
sively increases with time (suggesting the formation of a
passivation layer which continuously grows on the elec-
trode surface), in the two composite electrolytes the Ri
value does not vary consistently over a prolonged
period of time (suggesting the occurrence of a stable and
compact passivation layer). Such an interfacial stabili-
zing e†ect of the dispersed ceramic powders has recently
been conÐrmed by tests carried out in our own21 and
various other laboratories.22,23
Composite ‘dry’ PEO–LiX polymer electrolytes
It has been often postulated that the presence of any
liquid impurity (e.g. moisture, residual traces of the

trolytes. Data were obtatined by monitoring the impedance
response of symmetrical Li/electrolyte/Li cells maintained
under open circuit conditions at 85¡C.

tivity. A possible explanation for the enhancement of

Composite ‘dry’ PEO–LiX polymer electrolytes
It has been often postulated that the presence of any
liquid impurity (e.g. moisture, residual traces of the
Fig. 1. Time evolution of the lithium interfacial resistance Ri
trolytes. Data were obtatined by monitoring the impedance
response of symmetrical Li/electrolyte/Li cells maintained
under open circuit conditions at 85¡C.
carrier casting solvent, etc.) greatly deteriorates the con-
ditions of the lithium interface in PEOÈLiX polymer
electrolytes. This is reasonable because these impurities
are expected to react in a unpredictable way with
lithium giving rise to non-uniform, porous passivation
layers. Thus, in the production of PEO-based electro-
lytes the level of impurities should be reduced to a
minimum. This may be achieved either by selecting
solvent-free electrolyte preparation procedures or by the
addition of impurity trappers (see above) or, ultimately,
by a combination of the two actions. We have tried to
powder-supplemented composite electrolytes using a
procedure where the presence of any liquid was avoided
throughout the entire process. The procedure consisted
of the direct reaction of the electrolyte components
obtained by hot-pressing an intimate mixture of PEO,
LiX and the selected ceramic additive.24 This excludes
the presence of any residual solvent and gives uniform
composite electrolyte membranes. These “dryÏ mem-
branes have a conductivity similar to that o†ered by
their parent electrolytes prepared by the common
casting processes, but a dramatically higher interfacial
stability. Figure 2 reports in comparison the evolution
of the lithium interfacial resistance in a hot-pressed
composite PEO-based electrolyte and in an analogous
electrolyte prepared by casting. Clearly, the interfacial
resistance of the former remains stable for several days
at a very low value, i.e. around 150 ) cm2. This result
conÐrms that the removal of residual solvents and of
traces of liquid (e.g. water) impurities is indeed highly
beneÐcial in improving the stability of the lithium elec-
trode interface. Indeed, it is reasonable to assume that
in the absence of liquids, the lithium passivation process
may only be due to the reaction with the lithium salt

anion (in the case of Fig. 2, with the
which probably gives the formation of a thin, compact,
inorganic Ðlm (probably a Ñuoride-based Ðlm). This
type of Ðlm is considered to be the most favourable for
assuring lithium interface stability18 and this explains
the favourable properties of the dry composite electro-
In conclusion, the “dryÏ composite PEO-based elec-
trolytes seem to be quite promising for assuring favour-
able interfacial conditions and, thus, a good cyclability
of the lithium electrode. This in turn would suggest that
these electrolytes may proÐtably be used for the devel-
opment of rechargeable lithium polymer batteries. Pre-
liminary tests carried out in our laboratory seem to
conÐrm the validity of this suggestion.24
The desire to extend the use of polymer electrolytes to
devices capable of operating at ambient and sub-
ambient temperatures has motivated the search for
materials capable of conducting in a temperature range
wider than that of the PEOÈLiX complexes. The most
successful result has been the development of electrolyte
membranes formed by trapping liquid solutions (e.g.
solutions of lithium salts in organic solvent mixtures) in
varies from case to case and includes UV crosslinking,
casting and gelation, the latter being the most com-
monly adopted. There has been some discussion as to
whether gel electrolytes are to be considered as simple
two-phase compounds where the polymer is a passive
component acting as a rigid framework for regions of
liquid solutions, or whether they are integrated systems
where the polymer provides the stability of the gel
network down to the immediate vicinity of the Li`
ions.26 Recent results based on NMR and Raman
spectroscopy27 show that some interactions do indeed
occur between the polymer backbone and the electro-
lyte solution, to an extent which depends upon the
Gel-type electrolytes were originally introduced in
1975 by Feullade and Perche.28 However, the studies of
these materials have been increased recently with the
development and the characterization of many new
types having di†erent properties.29 Table 1 lists the
electrochemical characteristics of a few examples of gel
electrolytes developed in our laboratory. Clearly, the
gel-type electrolytes have quite promising properties in
solutions) and of anodic stability (to a level allowing the
use of high voltage cathodes). In principle, these proper-
ties make the electrolytes suitable for use in versatile,
Conducting polymers for rechargeable L i batteries

formances in terms of cyclability and energy density
have been obtained with lithium battery prototypes
based on gel electrolytes and a pPy organic cathode30
However, concern regarding the reliability of these
batteries cannot be excluded because passivation of
lithium is expected to be quite consistent in gel electro-
lytes due to the high content of liquid plasticizers.
Indeed, the occurrence of severe passivation phenomena
has been revealed by impedance analysis.32 Possible
approaches for improving the stability of the lithium/
gel-type electrolyte interface may be: (i) the selection of
poorly reactive solvents and/or polymer components
and (ii) the addition of ceramic powders cited above.
E†ectively, Fig. 3, which reports the evolution time of

in gel electrolytes based on the
di†ering by the type of immobilizing polymer (PAN or
PMMA) and by the addition of 10 wt% zeolite powders. Data
were obtained by monitoring the impedance response of sym-
metrical Li/electrolyte/Li cells maintained under open circuit
solution but di†ering in the type of
immobilizing solvent and in the addition of zeolite
approaches cited. However, the improvements, although
of interest, are not to the level of being totally satisfac-
tory. In contrast, it has to be pointed out that while the
wet-like conÐguration is essential to confer high con-
ductivity to the gel electrolytes, it unavoidably a†ects
the stability of the lithium metal interface. The conclu-
sion is that these electrolytes, rather than be used in
lithium metal polymer batteries, should be directed to
the development of new-design, plastic-like lithium ion
batteries, i.e. to batteries where the lithium anode is
replaced by a lithiated carbonaceous anode.33 In fact,
the wet nature of the gel electrolytes may be quite useful
for assuring electrode wettability and thus, good inter-
facial interconnections, with the high surface carbon-
In general, the replacement of common liquid electro-
lytes with an ionic membrane is an important techno-
because it provides the prospect of a favourable com-
bination of the high energy and long life typical of the
lithium ion process, with the reliability and easy pro-
cessability typical of plastic conÐgurations. A successful
achievement in this area has been the development of a
plastic lithium ion battery using an elasticized electro-
lyte membrane separator formed by a copolymer of
vinylidene Ñuoride and hexaÑuoropropylene (PVDFÈ
HFP).34 We have considered an alternative process
involving the direct lamination of the battery package
(carbon anode ÐlmÈelectrolyte membraneÈcathode Ðlm).
To assure good interfacial contact we used gel type elec-
trolyte membranes which, although macroscopically
stable, contain an excess of the liquid solution com-
ponent.35 A typical example is a LiClO4ÈECÈDECÈ
PAN electrolyte (see Table 1). Promising results have
indeed been obtained using this electrolyte as separ
ator in graphite-chromium stabilized manganese spinel
lithium ion plastic battery prototypes. A good fraction
of carbon capacity (maximum value 373 mA h g~1) is
delivered upon cycling; see Fig. 4.
Fig. 4. Delivered capacity (reported in terms of percent
C/LiClO4ÈECÈDMCÈPAN/LiCryMn2~yO4
lithium ion battery; room temperature; C/6 rate.
Another development in the area of plastic lithium
ion batteries which has been attempted in our labor-
atory concerns the use of very low cost cathodes, i.e.
polymers such as pPy or pAni. Typical examples
cyclic transfer of Li` ions between anode and cathode
in common lithium ion cells,33 the chargeÈdischarge
process of these cells involves the double intercalation
of the Li` cations at the graphite anode and of the
anions at the pPy cathode. Hence, these cells
have been named “dionÏ plastic batteries.36,37 Figure 5
shows that the dion battery is characterized by a good
cyclability, and thus that it may be of some practical
interest. The main advantages are its low cost and its
compatibility with the environment. Another favourable
aspect is that, if properly designed, the dion batteries
are capable of delivering reasonably high power pulses.
lithium ion batteries have been discussed above, and are
the lower capacity and thus the lower energy density.
dion battery; room temperature; 0É022 mA cm~2 rate.
Both PEO-based and gel-type polymer electrolytes are
of practical interest, the former for the development of
high-energy polymer lithium batteries and the latter for
new-design, plastic lithium ion batteries. In addition to
batteries, gel electrolytes may be successfully used for
the fabrication of high-power redox supercapacitors5
and laminated electrochromic windows.7
The results reported here have been obtained thanks to
the Ðnancial support from NEDO, Japan (International
Project) and CNR, Italy (Progetto Strategico Batterie
Leggere). Thanks are also expressed to G. B. Appe-
tecchi, F. Bonino, F. Croce, F. Gerace, S. Panero, F.
Ronci and E. Spila for their experimental work.
